---
schema: verdi.deviation/v1
covers: 8301439b160652a960b85d13c2b2567ff5eebe3c
findings:
  - { id: judged-ac-4-static-guard-weaker-than-claim, kind: judged, text: "The docs change itself satisfies ac-4 (section D.5 now names `verdi disposition` as the only sanctioned mechanism, and the current document contains no hand-editing language), but the static evidence backing the negative half of the AC is weaker than it claims: TestArchitectureDoc_NamesDispositionVerb guards against hand-editing prose via five literal substrings, two of which (\"edit ... by hand\", \"editing ... by hand\") contain a literal \" ... \" and can never match real prose, and none of which would catch a paraphrase such as 'update the disposition fields yourself in an editor'. The obligation record (disposition-verb/ac-4--static) presents this as proof that no sentence implies hand-editing, which the phrase list cannot actually establish — the AC holds today, but the guard would not detect a future regression expressed in different words. (confidence 0.45)" }
  - { id: judged-ac-1-byte-for-byte-is-rerender-not-splice, kind: judged, text: "ac-1's 'carried over byte-for-byte' is implemented as a full deterministic re-render (artifact.DecodeDeviation → value-copy → align.RenderMarkdown) rather than a byte-preserving splice of the untouched frontmatter. For reports produced by `verdi align` itself this is byte-identical, and the AC's two operative verifier claims (stored digest still matches ComputeDigest; VerifyIntegrity still succeeds) hold regardless because field values are preserved — the behavioral test proves both. But a strictly-valid living report whose frontmatter formatting differs from the renderer's canonical style (still passing strict decode) would have every field, including digest:/integrity:/judge_integrity: lines and all OTHER findings' entries, silently reformatted on write — a byte-level change to content the AC says is carried over byte-for-byte. Notably the body side IS held to the byte standard (the count==1 exact-line check refuses with exit 2 on any drift), so the frontmatter is the one place where the verb normalizes rather than refuses. (confidence 0.30)" }
digest: sha256:284457ee3d7538b34d78b0b70accd02edd807b7724531e1d4487560822413385
integrity: sha256:83e3dfe72e289090ae8b2d387d79f0b58e2c0b252d77255c306d0292e1203466
judge_integrity: { stdin_b64: , raw_result: "```json\n{\"findings\":[{\"id\":\"ac-4-static-guard-weaker-than-claim\",\"text\":\"The docs change itself satisfies ac-4 (section D.5 now names `verdi disposition` as the only sanctioned mechanism, and the current document contains no hand-editing language), but the static evidence backing the negative half of the AC is weaker than it claims: TestArchitectureDoc_NamesDispositionVerb guards against hand-editing prose via five literal substrings, two of which (\\\"edit ... by hand\\\", \\\"editing ... by hand\\\") contain a literal \\\" ... \\\" and can never match real prose, and none of which would catch a paraphrase such as 'update the disposition fields yourself in an editor'. The obligation record (disposition-verb/ac-4--static) presents this as proof that no sentence implies hand-editing, which the phrase list cannot actually establish — the AC holds today, but the guard would not detect a future regression expressed in different words.\",\"confidence\":0.45},{\"id\":\"ac-1-byte-for-byte-is-rerender-not-splice\",\"text\":\"ac-1's 'carried over byte-for-byte' is implemented as a full deterministic re-render (artifact.DecodeDeviation → value-copy → align.RenderMarkdown) rather than a byte-preserving splice of the untouched frontmatter. For reports produced by `verdi align` itself this is byte-identical, and the AC's two operative verifier claims (stored digest still matches ComputeDigest; VerifyIntegrity still succeeds) hold regardless because field values are preserved — the behavioral test proves both. But a strictly-valid living report whose frontmatter formatting differs from the renderer's canonical style (still passing strict decode) would have every field, including digest:/integrity:/judge_integrity: lines and all OTHER findings' entries, silently reformatted on write — a byte-level change to content the AC says is carried over byte-for-byte. Notably the body side IS held to the byte standard (the count==1 exact-line check refuses with exit 2 on any drift), so the frontmatter is the one place where the verb normalizes rather than refuses.\",\"confidence\":0.3}]}\n```" }
provenance: { generator: verdi-align, version: v0, inputs: [spec/disposition-verb@8301439b160652a960b85d13c2b2567ff5eebe3c, spec/disposition-verb@d2ecf50f0e6f8a3163692abce22fe55de7adf3c2], digest: sha256:284457ee3d7538b34d78b0b70accd02edd807b7724531e1d4487560822413385, integrity: sha256:83e3dfe72e289090ae8b2d387d79f0b58e2c0b252d77255c306d0292e1203466 }

# Alignment report

## Computed

(none)

### Boundary diff vs acceptance baseline

(no impacted services)

### Diagram alignment

- (no accepted proposals)
- (no illustrative diagrams in this spec's body)

## Judged

- **judged-ac-4-static-guard-weaker-than-claim** [UNDISPOSITIONED]: The docs change itself satisfies ac-4 (section D.5 now names `verdi disposition` as the only sanctioned mechanism, and the current document contains no hand-editing language), but the static evidence backing the negative half of the AC is weaker than it claims: TestArchitectureDoc_NamesDispositionVerb guards against hand-editing prose via five literal substrings, two of which ("edit ... by hand", "editing ... by hand") contain a literal " ... " and can never match real prose, and none of which would catch a paraphrase such as 'update the disposition fields yourself in an editor'. The obligation record (disposition-verb/ac-4--static) presents this as proof that no sentence implies hand-editing, which the phrase list cannot actually establish — the AC holds today, but the guard would not detect a future regression expressed in different words. (confidence 0.45)
- **judged-ac-1-byte-for-byte-is-rerender-not-splice** [UNDISPOSITIONED]: ac-1's 'carried over byte-for-byte' is implemented as a full deterministic re-render (artifact.DecodeDeviation → value-copy → align.RenderMarkdown) rather than a byte-preserving splice of the untouched frontmatter. For reports produced by `verdi align` itself this is byte-identical, and the AC's two operative verifier claims (stored digest still matches ComputeDigest; VerifyIntegrity still succeeds) hold regardless because field values are preserved — the behavioral test proves both. But a strictly-valid living report whose frontmatter formatting differs from the renderer's canonical style (still passing strict decode) would have every field, including digest:/integrity:/judge_integrity: lines and all OTHER findings' entries, silently reformatted on write — a byte-level change to content the AC says is carried over byte-for-byte. Notably the body side IS held to the byte standard (the count==1 exact-line check refuses with exit 2 on any drift), so the frontmatter is the one place where the verb normalizes rather than refuses. (confidence 0.30)
